hero.tagline: Vermont's small claims court offers a streamlined process for resolving monetary disputes up to $10,000, with lower caps for certain debt types.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: For general claims; $5,000 for medical debt and consumer credit transactions

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Depends on claim amount and type

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds the $10,000 limit, you may choose to waive the excess and file in small claims court, or file in the Civil Division for the full amount. Splitting one dispute into multiple claims to fit under the cap is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $10,000. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $3,000 to a colleague who agreed to repay in installments but stopped after the first payment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any repayment history.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 but failed to make any payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $4,500, invoiced the client, but haven't received payment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Submit the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $3,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in unpaid rent over the past three months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods with a $1,200 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Demand letter required before filing. Include the bounced check and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $50 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $6,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now you're seeking repayment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the loan agreement and proof of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont's small claims court addresses various consumer disputes, with a statute of limitations of 6 years for most claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $800 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair date. Present repair invoices and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Incomplete or poor-quality contractor work
claims.example: A contractor was paid $5,000 to renovate your bathroom but left the job unfinished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from contract date. Bring the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,200 for an event, but they never showed up and won't refund.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from event date. Provide the service agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your card for three months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from first unauthorized charge. Bring cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Provide the sales contract, any warranties, and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product as 'all-natural,' but it contains synthetic ingredients, and you paid $200 based on the claim.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Bring the advertisement and proof of purchase.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont's small claims court handles property damage claims up to $10,000, with a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $4,000 in damages, and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Provide police reports, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your shed during a storm, causing $3,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Bring photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $2,000 worth of damage to your apartment beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery of damage. Provide the lease agreement, photos, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog chewed up your $1,500 outdoor furniture.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Bring photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your bicycle with a friend who returned it with $800 in damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide evidence of the agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont's small claims court addresses monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $10,000, excluding eviction cases.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification after you moved out.
claims.notes: Tenant must provide a forwarding address within 30 days of moving out. Landlord has 14 days to return the deposit or provide an itemized statement of deductions.

claims.name: Landlord failed to provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord kept $500 of your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlord must provide an itemized statement within 14 days. Failure to do so may forfeit their right to withhold any portion of the deposit.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: Tenant may be entitled to damages for breach of the warranty of habitability. Document all communications and conditions.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's personal property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $2,000.
claims.notes: Landlord must follow proper procedures for handling abandoned property. Failure to do so may result in liability.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal in Vermont. Tenants may sue for damages resulting from unlawful eviction.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but moved out early, and the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: Landlord must return any unearned rent. Document the lease terms and any communications.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months' rent totaling $2,400.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You fixed a broken heater for $500 after the landlord failed to do so, and deducted it from rent, but the landlord is suing for nonpayment.
claims.notes: Tenants must follow proper procedures for repair and deduct. Keep all receipts and document communications.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont's small claims court handles wage-related disputes up to $10,000, with a 2-year statute of limitations for unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,000 in unpaid wages over several pay periods.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date wages were due. Provide pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,200.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date wages were due. Document resignation and any communications.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: Your employer owes you $4,500 in earned commissions but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date commissions were due. Provide commission agreements and sales records.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime but were paid at your regular rate instead of time-and-a-half.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date overtime was due. Document hours worked and pay received.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont's small claims court addresses personal claims up to $10,000, with varying statutes of limitations depending on the claim type.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on an unmarked wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your camera worth $1,500, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from demand for return. Document the loan agreement and any communications.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your lawnmower worth $800 and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from theft date. Provide evidence of ownership and any communications.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Vermont's small claims court handles vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000, with a 3-year statute of limitations for property damage.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs you didn't authorize.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Provide repair invoices and any communications.

claims.name: Vehicle wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you had to pay $300 to retrieve it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from tow date. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot handle eviction cases; these must be filed in the Civil Division.
Disputes involving ownership or title to real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims for slander or libel are excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the monetary jurisdiction and complexity suitable for small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are not handled in small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Family Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Probate Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot hear cases against federal entities; such claims must be filed in federal court.
Class action lawsuits are beyond the procedural scope of small claims court.
Small claims court can only award monetary damages and cannot order someone to do or stop doing something.
Disputes over workers' compensation benefits are handled by the Department of Labor, not small claims court.
Bankruptcy cases are under federal jurisdiction and cannot be filed in small claims court.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are typically not eligible for small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Arbitration as specified in the contract

exclusions.explanation: Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Probate Division of the Superior Court

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in Vermont small claims court; only actual monetary losses can be recovered.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 2x check amount or $100, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $50 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in small claims court if specified by statute or contract.

filing_options: County where the defendant lives or has a business
Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Vermont offers an eFiling system for small claims cases, accessible through the judiciary's website.

service_fees.notes: Cost depends on current postal rates.
Fees set by the sheriff's department.

forms.number: Application to Waive Filing Fees and Service Costs
forms.name: Application to Waive Filing Fees and Service Costs

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are typically added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment against the loser.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
Date deposit was due to be returned

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a written demand for payment must be sent, and if unpaid after 30 days, statutory penalties may apply.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but documenting requests for payment is advisable.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Tenants should provide a forwarding address within 30 days of moving out to facilitate the return of the security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting complaints and communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Small Claims Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Application to Waive Filing Fees and Service Costs
forms.description: Requests a waiver of filing and service fees based on financial hardship.

forms.name: Small Claims Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's complaint.

forms.name: Small Claims Counterclaim
forms.description: Enables the defendant to file a counterclaim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Small Claims Motion
forms.description: Used to request the court to take a specific action in a small claims case.

forms.name: Small Claims Judgment Order
forms.description: The court's official decision in a small claims case.

forms.name: Small Claims Financial Disclosure
forms.description: Requires the judgment debtor to disclose financial information to aid in judgment collection.

forms.name: Small Claims Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Small Claims Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a small claims judgment.

forms.name: Small Claims Writ of Execution
forms.description: Authorizes the sheriff to enforce a judgment by seizing property or wages.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: The court clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail.
cons: May be less reliable if defendant avoids mail

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: A sheriff personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the summons and complaint.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent; if none, serve an officer or managing agent.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Vermont rules and the rules of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file an answer within 30 days of service; failure to do so may result in a default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant does not respond or appear, the court may issue a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even in default cases, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 30 days; if denied, the decision can be appealed.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to the Civil Division.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case and evidence.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court as soon as possible before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts may offer mediation services before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is based on the existing record; no new evidence is presented.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but it is advisable to first file a motion to vacate.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a motion for trustee process against earnings; if granted, serve the employer, who will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages and remit to the court.

forms.name: Motion for Trustee Process Against Earnings

methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it on the bank, which will freeze and remit funds up to the judgment amount.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains sufficient funds in bank accounts.
exemptions: Certain amounts may be exempt under state law

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the town clerk where the property is located; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective for securing payment, especially if debtor plans to sell property.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize seizure of debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, which allows the sheriff to seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.
exemptions: Certain personal property may be exempt under state law

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; if granted, the judgment is extended for another 8 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized based on the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Certificate of Assumed Business Name with the Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may authorize an employee to appear on their behalf.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts in court.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors, use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the statute of limitations for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the case may be dismissed or delayed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Vermont's rules for service of process carefully; consider using the sheriff or a professional process server.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: The case may be transferred or dismissed, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: File in the county where the defendant lives or has a business, or where the cause of action arose.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; collection can be challenging.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to take post-judgment collection actions like wage garnishment or bank levies.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking exemptions in collection
pitfalls.what_happens: Certain debtor assets are protected from collection, limiting recovery options.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Understand Vermont's exemption laws to assess the feasibility of collecting on a judgment.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Vermont small claims court. The process is designed to be simple and accessible for individuals representing themselves. However, you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but typically, a small claims case in Vermont is resolved within 30 to 60 days from filing to judgment. Factors such as court scheduling and whether the defendant contests the claim can affect the duration.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Vermont, you can sue for up to $10,000 in small claims court. For claims involving medical debt or consumer credit transactions, the limit is $5,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear in court after being properly served, the judge may issue a default judgment in your favor. You may still need to provide evidence to support your claim before the judgment is granted.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment to the Civil Division of the Superior Court. The appeal must be filed within 30 days of the judgment, and a filing fee applies.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: In Vermont, a small claims judgment is valid for 8 years. If the judgment is not satisfied within that period, you may need to take additional legal steps to renew it.

state_specific_notes: Vermont allows eFiling for small claims cases through the judiciary's website.
Small claims court cannot order someone to do something; it can only award monetary damages.
The court may offer mediation services to help parties reach a settlement before trial.
If a claim exceeds the small claims limit, you may waive the excess and file in small claims court, or file in the Civil Division for the full amount.
Vermont's small claims court is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves, with simplified procedures and forms.

interesting_findings: Vermont's small claims court has a lower cap of $5,000 for medical debt and consumer credit transactions, distinguishing it from the general $10,000 limit.
The state offers a comprehensive set of forms and instructions to assist self-represented litigants in navigating the small claims process.

sources.label: Vt. Stat. Ann. Title 12 Chapter 187 (Small Claims)
Vt. Stat. Ann. Title 12 Chapter 23 (Limitation of actions)
Vt. Stat. Ann. Title 9 Ch. 63 (Consumer Protection Act)
Vt. Stat. Ann. Title 9 § 2311 (Bad check civil penalty)
